findings.description: R-3 (single shared PostgreSQL database with per-service schemas) directly conflicts with R-7 (each service must own its data and not share database tables). These are two opposing architectural sides — shared-database monolith vs. true microservice data isolation — and the document tries to hold both simultaneously without acknowledging the trade-off.
findings.evidence: R-3: 'All application data shall be stored in a single PostgreSQL 15 database with logical partitioning per service schema.' R-7: 'Each service must own its data and shall not share database tables with other services.' A single shared database instance is a deployment-level coupling that microservice data-ownership principles are designed to eliminate. 'Logical partitioning per schema' is a compromise that keeps the operational costs of both approaches (shared infra contention, but also no cross-service joins allowed), making it a false middle rather than a real integration.

findings.description: R-1 (mandatory 6-service microservice architecture with Kubernetes) and R-2 (Kafka with 3-broker cluster) are solution prescriptions stated as requirements without any problem statement, scale figure, or load justification in the background. The background only says 'growing' and 'at scale' — no numbers, no current pain. This is solution-first thinking masquerading as requirements.
findings.evidence: Background states 'growing and needs a modern platform to handle orders at scale' — no current order volume, no projected scale, no identified bottleneck. R-1 mandates 'at least 6 separate services' and Kubernetes. R-2 mandates Kafka with a specific cluster topology. These are infrastructure decisions that should follow from a problem definition. The real tension (simplicity/speed-to-market vs. scalability/resilience) is never surfaced, so no conscious choice was made about which side should dominate.

findings.description: R-8 mandates Redis with a minimum of 16 GB allocated memory for 'all caching needs' with no justification of what is being cached, what hit-rate is expected, or what data volume warrants this floor. This is a solution specification without a defined problem.
findings.evidence: R-8: 'Redis 7.x shall be used for all caching needs with a minimum of 16GB allocated memory.' No cache use-case is described anywhere in the document. The 16 GB floor is an arbitrary implementation detail presented as a requirement.

findings.description: R-5 specifies the exact frontend stack (React 18, TypeScript, Next.js with SSR) without any stated UI/UX problem, user type, SEO requirement, or performance constraint that would drive the SSR choice. This forecloses architectural options without a stated rationale.
findings.evidence: R-5: 'The frontend shall be built using React 18 with TypeScript and Next.js for server-side rendering.' No context about who uses the frontend (internal ops users vs. public customers), no SEO or first-paint requirements — contexts where SSR vs. SPA decisions are driven by real trade-offs.

findings.description: R-2 mandates all inter-service communication through Kafka, while R-7 says services communicate 'through published APIs and events.' These are different communication models (async-only Kafka vs. synchronous APIs plus events) and the conflict is never resolved.
findings.evidence: R-2: 'All inter-service communication shall go through Kafka topics.' R-7: 'Services communicate only through published APIs and events.' 'Published APIs' implies synchronous request/response (REST, gRPC, etc.), which contradicts the Kafka-only mandate. No exception or priority ordering is given.
